mode: MODE 2 — cleanup (all remaining critical/major with win_id are patched; patching WINs with fewest open issues)

patches.find: a dimensional error that undermines the claim before we even assess the derivation.
patches.replace: a dimensional error that undermines the claim before we even assess the derivation. Even correcting the units charitably from rad/s² to rad/s, the dome’s value 4.87×10⁻¹² falls 37% below Earth’s measured precession angular velocity of 7.73×10⁻¹² rad/s — the prediction fails on value even after the unit correction is applied. The dome’s own monitoring system (monitor.py) does not include gyroscopic precession in its 39-domain automated audit engine, confirming that even the model’s author does not treat this as a computationally validated prediction.
patches.rationale: ISS-175: Add the 37% numerical discrepancy (even with corrected units the dome value is wrong) and note that monitor.py does not validate this WIN.

patches.find: No testable prediction or measurable consequence follows from this claim.
patches.replace: No testable prediction or measurable consequence follows from this claim. The dome’s own automated monitoring system (monitor.py) does not include WIN-032 in its 39-domain audit engine — if the author considered this a genuine, testable prediction, it would appear in the monitoring pipeline. Its complete absence from the code is a tacit admission that no computational verification is possible for a theological axiom.
patches.rationale: ISS-195: Add the monitoring gap: monitor.py doesn't validate WIN-032, confirming the author doesn't treat it as a testable claim.

patches.find: The dome's local moon at ~2,534 km creates the same pattern problem as M2 and S2: the tidal force is concentrated in a sharp spike directly beneath the moon (dropping to 9% at 5,000 km offset, 0.6% at the equator). There is no far-side bulge — the disc edge is 8× farther than the moon's altitude.
patches.replace: The dome's local moon at ~2,534 km creates the K1-specific pattern problem: K1 amplitude is largest at high latitudes where the combined moon-sun declination effects are strongest, and near zero at the equator. A local moon at 2,534 km with ~28° declination would concentrate K1 amplitude along its sub-lunar track, becoming negligible beyond ~5,000 km. Yet Antarctic stations — which lie more than 15,000 km from the sub-lunar track on the dome's geometry — show coherent K1 amplitudes fully consistent with Arctic stations. There is no far-side mechanism; the disc edge is 8× farther than the moon's altitude.
patches.rationale: ISS-236: Replace vague 'same pattern problem as M2 and S2' with K1-specific latitude argument: K1 amplitude distribution directly contradicts a local moon because Antarctic stations (15,000+ km from sub-lunar track) show full K1 amplitudes.

patches.find: Note: WIN-011/012 (Mohe 1997 + BOU 2017) and WIN-014 (China SG 2009) use different instruments at different eclipses — the 'single dataset' framing is imprecise; the logical issue is claiming credit for opposite outcomes (positive and null) without advance specification.
patches.replace: Note: WIN-011/012 (Mohe 1997 + BOU 2017) and WIN-014 (China SG 2009) use different instruments, different continents, and different eclipses separated by twelve years. The logical issue is that the model claims credit for opposite outcomes — a positive gravity anomaly in WIN-011 and a null result in WIN-014 — without specifying in advance which location should yield which result. Without a prior prediction distinguishing eclipse-sensitive from insensitive sites, any outcome confirms the model.
patches.rationale: ISS-301: Remove self-referential 'framing is imprecise' note. Replace with clear language about why claiming credit for opposite outcomes is unfalsifiable.

patches.find: WIN-041 reprocesses the same INTERMAGNET time series (Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek, etc.) also used in WIN-004, WIN-005, WIN-035, and WIN-037.
patches.replace: WIN-041 reprocesses INTERMAGNET time series from the SAA region (stations including Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek, and related sites as referenced in the ECM V51.0 documentation) also cited across WIN-004, WIN-005, WIN-035, and WIN-037.
patches.rationale: ISS-041: Add qualifier that station names are inferred from ECM V51.0 documentation rather than independently verified.

patches.find: WIN-041 uses identical INTERMAGNET data as WIN-004/005/035/037; same data cannot confirm multiple independent WINs.
patches.replace: WIN-041 draws from the same INTERMAGNET network as WIN-004/005/035/037. Even when different stations are cited, multiple SAA stations within the anomaly’s ~5,000–8,000 km spatial correlation length are not independent measurements — they are correlated samples of a single geophysical anomaly. Claiming each station as a separate confirmation is equivalent to counting each thermometer in a warm room as independent proof the room is warm.
patches.rationale: ISS-332: Address the 'different stations' defense with the spatial correlation argument: stations within the SAA's correlation length are not statistically independent.

patches.find: Membach SG (superior instrument) found 0.0 uGal; result unconfirmed
patches.replace: Van Camp et al. (2001) found no eclipse gravity signal with four superconducting gravimeters; marginal spring-gravimeter detection remains unconfirmed
patches.rationale: ISS-080: Replace oversimplified '0.0 uGal' with accurate description: Van Camp found no signal above the noise floor, not a definitive zero measurement.

patches.find: The dome model's −6.5 μGal comes from this study but misrepresents its timing: gravitational shielding would peak at maximum eclipse (maximum coverage), not at the contacts.
patches.replace: The dome model’s −6.5 μGal comes from this study but misrepresents its timing: gravitational shielding would peak at maximum eclipse (maximum coverage), not at the contacts. A subsequent reanalysis (Yang & Wang, 2002, Astrophys. Space Sci. 282, 245–253) found the anomaly evidence weaker than the initial report once additional measurement controls were applied.
patches.rationale: ISS-081: Add Yang & Wang 2002 reanalysis reference showing the initial Wang et al. 2000 result didn't hold up under further scrutiny.

patches.find: The linear radial average gives H_eff ≈ 3,300 km → f = c/(4 × 3,300) = 22.6 Hz; the area-weighted average gives H_eff ≈ 2,100 km → 35 Hz.
patches.replace: Integrating H(r) = 8537·e^(−r/8619) analytically: the linear radial average gives H_eff = (H₀·L/R)(1−e^(−R/L)) ≈ 3,316 km → f = c/(4 × 3,316) = 22.6 Hz; the area-weighted average (proper for a disc) gives H_eff ≈ 2,140 km → 35 Hz. (Here H₀ = 8537 km, L = 8619 km, R = 20,015 km.)
patches.rationale: ISS-148: Add explicit analytical integral formulas for both averaging methods so the computation is verifiable.

patches.find: standard ionospheric physics (Sentman, 1995) reproduces 7.83 Hz and all five Schumann harmonics from measured ionospheric conductivity profiles.
patches.replace: standard ionospheric physics (Sentman, 1995, in Volland Handbook of Atmospheric Electrodynamics) reproduces 7.83 Hz and all five Schumann harmonics by solving Maxwell’s equations in a spherical shell with measured ionospheric conductivity profiles — no dome geometry required.
patches.rationale: ISS-149: Specify Sentman 1995 source and mechanism more precisely so a defender cannot claim the citation is vague.

patches.find: Working geophysicists deliberately avoid this noisy frequency band
patches.replace: The claimed ~12 Hz peak is the inter-harmonic spectral minimum between Schumann modes — a trough predicted by spherical-cavity physics, not a dome resonance peak
patches.rationale: ISS-155+156 (combined): The finding field incorrectly invokes the MT dead band (which is 1-5 kHz, not ~12 Hz). Replace with accurate description of the inter-harmonic minimum.

patches.find: This coupling constant is derived from the claimed Mohe density anomaly, which lacks independent confirmation from modern gravity surveys (EGM2020 satellite gravity model, ground gravity networks).
patches.replace: This coupling constant is derived from the Mohe eclipse gravity anomaly (Wang et al., 2000, Phys. Rev. D 62, 041101(R) — a peer-reviewed Rapid Communication), which nevertheless lacks independent confirmation from modern gravity surveys (EGM2020 satellite gravity model, ground gravity networks).
patches.rationale: ISS-159: Acknowledge Wang et al. 2000 as a peer-reviewed publication (Rapid Communication in Phys Rev D) while maintaining the critique of lack of independent confirmation.

patches.replace:
See Section 3.5.8.

Internal contradiction with WIN-013/014. The dome’s own WIN-013 and WIN-014 claim null eclipse gravity results as model confirmations. But κ is defined as the ratio of magnetic anomaly to gravity anomaly — if the eclipse gravity anomaly is zero as WIN-013/014 claim, the denominator vanishes and κ is undefined. The model cannot simultaneously claim that eclipse gravity anomalies both exist (WIN-012) and don’t exist (WIN-013/014).
patches.rationale: ISS-160: Add the WIN-013/014 internal contradiction as the sharpest argument: the model's three WINs are mutually exclusive.

stale_issues_verified_fixed.note: Current WIN-008 detail_evidence uses precise 'inter-harmonic power minimum' language and does not conflate MT dead band (800 Hz-5 kHz) with ~12 Hz region. Issue pre-dates current text; text already correct.
Current WIN-008 detail_evidence does not use 'attenuation valley' or spectrum-inversion language. The 14.3 Hz disproof and spherical-cavity prediction are both present. Issue already addressed.
